enrichment.short_description: WebEngage is a full-stack customer engagement and retention platform that helps consumer brands build customer data profiles, design automated journeys, and send personalized messages across email, SMS, WhatsApp, push, and in-app channels.
enrichment.seo_title: WebEngage Pricing, Features, and Alternatives (2026 Guide)
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how WebEngage helps consumer brands with customer data, journey orchestration, and omnichannel messaging, plus its pricing model and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WebEngage is a full-stack customer engagement and retention platform founded in 2011 and headquartered in Mumbai, India, with additional offices across India and international hubs including Dubai. The company was built to help consumer-facing brands, particularly in e-commerce, edtech, fintech, media, and gaming, unify customer data, orchestrate marketing journeys, and deliver personalized messages at scale. At its core, WebEngage combines a customer data platform with a visual journey designer and an omnichannel messaging engine spanning email, SMS, WhatsApp, mobile push, web push, in-app messages, and on-site personalization widgets. Marketing and growth teams use WebEngage to segment users based on behavioral and transactional data, trigger automated lifecycle campaigns such as cart abandonment, onboarding, or re-engagement flows, and run A/B tests to optimize conversion and retention. The platform includes analytics dashboards for funnels, cohorts, and campaign performance, alongside a recommendation engine for product and content personalization. WebEngage positions itself as a mid-market alternative to larger enterprise customer engagement suites such as Braze and Salesforce Marketing Cloud, and it competes closely with MoEngage and CleverTap, particularly among growth-stage consumer tech companies in India, Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America. The company reports serving several hundred brands globally and has raised more than 30 million dollars in venture funding across multiple rounds. WebEngage does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers must request a demo and receive a custom quote based on monthly active users, message volume, and the specific channels and features required, which makes upfront cost comparison harder than with more transparent competitors. Implementation typically involves onboarding support from WebEngage's customer success team to configure data pipelines, integrate SDKs into mobile and web properties, and set up initial journeys. Strengths commonly cited by users include the depth of its journey builder, the breadth of supported channels, especially WhatsApp, which matters for markets like India and Southeast Asia, and responsive support. Common criticisms include a steeper learning curve for non-technical marketers, occasional documentation gaps, and pricing opacity. For a directory audience evaluating customer engagement software, WebEngage is best understood as a purpose-built retention marketing platform for consumer apps and e-commerce businesses that need deep behavioral segmentation and multi-channel automation rather than a general-purpose email marketing tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WebEngage
paragraphs: WebEngage is a customer engagement and retention platform founded in 2011 and headquartered in Mumbai, India. It was built for consumer-facing companies that need to turn raw user behavior into personalized, automated marketing across many channels at once.

The platform combines a customer data layer with a visual journey builder, letting teams define triggers such as a cart abandonment or an app install, and then route users through branching, multi-step campaigns that adjust based on their behavior.

WebEngage is used heavily by e-commerce, edtech, fintech, gaming, and media companies across India, Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America, where it competes with MoEngage, CleverTap, and larger enterprise suites like Braze.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: WebEngage's journey designer lets marketers build automated, multi-channel campaigns without writing code, combining email, SMS, WhatsApp, mobile push, web push, in-app messages, and on-site widgets in a single flow.

A built-in analytics layer tracks funnels, cohorts, and campaign-level metrics, while an A/B testing engine and a recommendation engine help teams optimize messaging and personalize product or content suggestions.

Native WhatsApp support is a notable differentiator compared to many Western-built engagement platforms, reflecting WebEngage's strength in markets where WhatsApp is a primary customer communication channel.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: WebEngage does not publish self-serve pricing on its website. Instead, the pricing page routes prospects to a demo request, and a Marketing Automation Expert works out a custom quote.

Cost is typically driven by factors such as monthly active users or contacts reached, message volume across channels, and which features, such as advanced personalization or premium support, are included.

This sales-led pricing model makes WebEngage harder to comparison-shop against competitors with transparent tiers, though it also allows contracts to be tailored to a company's specific scale and channel mix.

features.title: Customer Data Platform
features.description: Unifies behavioral, transactional, and demographic data into a single customer profile used for segmentation and personalization.

features.title: Journey Designer
features.description: Visual, trigger-based builder for creating multi-step, cross-channel automated marketing journeys.

features.title: Omnichannel Messaging
features.description: Sends campaigns across email, SMS, WhatsApp, mobile push, web push, and in-app messages from one platform.

features.title: Personalization and Recommendations
features.description: A recommendation engine suggests products or content tailored to each user's behavior.

features.title: Analytics and Funnels
features.description: Dashboards for tracking conversion funnels, cohorts, and campaign performance over time.

features.title: A/B Testing
features.description: Built-in experimentation tools for testing messaging, timing, and journey variations.

features.title: WhatsApp Marketing
features.description: Native WhatsApp campaign and automation support, important for markets across Asia, the Middle East, and Latin America.

features.title: SDKs and Integrations
features.description: Mobile and web SDKs plus integrations with common data warehouses and marketing tools for data sync.

pros: Deep behavioral segmentation and journey orchestration built for consumer apps
Strong multi-channel coverage including WhatsApp, uncommon in many Western-built tools
Positioned as a more affordable mid-market alternative to Braze or Salesforce Marketing Cloud
Dedicated onboarding and customer success support for new accounts
cons: No published self-serve pricing, requiring a sales conversation for any quote
Steeper learning curve for marketers without technical support
Some users report documentation gaps compared to newer competitors
Best suited to consumer-scale user bases, less cost-effective for very small teams

faqs.question: What is WebEngage used for?
faqs.answer: WebEngage is used by consumer brands to unify customer data and run automated, personalized marketing campaigns across email, SMS, WhatsApp, push, and in-app channels.

faqs.question: How much does WebEngage cost?
faqs.answer: WebEngage does not publish pricing publicly. Cost is quoted individually based on usage such as monthly active users and message volume, so you need to request a demo for a price.

faqs.question: Does WebEngage offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: WebEngage does not advertise a public self-serve free tier; a trial or proof-of-concept can typically be arranged by contacting its sales team.

faqs.question: What channels does WebEngage support?
faqs.answer: WebEngage supports email, SMS, WhatsApp, mobile push, web push, in-app messages, and on-site personalization widgets.

faqs.question: Who are WebEngage's main competitors?
faqs.answer: WebEngage is most often compared to MoEngage, CleverTap, Braze, and Salesforce Marketing Cloud.

faqs.question: Is WebEngage suitable for small businesses?
faqs.answer: WebEngage is generally aimed at growth-stage and larger consumer tech companies with meaningful user volume rather than very small businesses, given its custom, sales-led pricing.

faqs.question: Does WebEngage support WhatsApp marketing?
faqs.answer: Yes, native WhatsApp campaigns and automation are a core part of the platform's omnichannel offering.

faqs.question: Where is WebEngage headquartered?
faqs.answer: WebEngage is headquartered in Mumbai, India, with additional offices in India and international hubs such as Dubai.

use_cases.title: E-commerce retention marketing
use_cases.description: Automating cart abandonment, browse abandonment, and win-back campaigns across email, push, and WhatsApp.

use_cases.title: Edtech and fintech onboarding automation
use_cases.description: Guiding new users through activation and feature adoption with triggered, behavior-based journeys.

use_cases.title: Media, OTT, and gaming engagement
use_cases.description: Re-engaging inactive users and promoting new content or in-app events through personalized push and in-app messaging.

features: Omnichannel messaging across email, SMS, WhatsApp, push, and in-app

enrichment.short_description: Webflow is a visual, no-code website builder and CMS that gives designers full control over responsive HTML and CSS, with built-in hosting, a content management system, and ecommerce tools.
enrichment.seo_title: Webflow Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Webflow's visual website builder, CMS, and ecommerce tools, its 2026 pricing plans, and how it compares to Wix, Squarespace, and other no-code builders.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Webflow is a visual, no-code website builder and content management system founded in 2012 and headquartered in San Francisco, California, having gone through Y Combinator in its early days. Unlike drag-and-drop builders that generate proprietary or bloated markup, Webflow lets designers visually construct pages while the platform outputs clean, semantic HTML and CSS in the background, giving marketing teams and agencies production-grade sites without writing code, alongside the option to add custom code or code components for advanced needs. The platform bundles a visual canvas, a flexible CMS for blogs, directories, and other structured content, built-in hosting on a global CDN, interactions and animation tools, forms, SEO controls, localization for multilingual sites, and increasingly, AI-assisted site generation and content tools. Webflow Ecommerce adds product catalogs, cart, and checkout functionality, though it is generally considered less mature than dedicated platforms like Shopify. Webflow has grown into one of the most widely used tools among digital agencies, in-house marketing teams, and freelance designers who need more design flexibility than Squarespace or Wix but do not want to manage a full custom development stack or a WordPress installation. In May 2026, Webflow significantly restructured its pricing, consolidating its older CMS and Business site plans into a single Premium tier and introducing a new Team plan aimed at organizations that have outgrown self-serve usage. As of the update, Site plans include a free Starter tier, a Basic plan around 15 dollars per month billed yearly for simpler sites, and a Premium plan around 25 dollars per month billed yearly that adds CMS collections, site search, and higher bandwidth and page limits; an Enterprise tier remains custom-priced for large organizations with advanced security, single sign-on, and support needs. Separately, Workspace seats, Full, Limited, and Free, control how many people can edit or collaborate on projects, and AI credits are now bundled into all Workspace plans. Common praise for Webflow centers on its unmatched design flexibility for a no-code tool, strong CMS capabilities for content-heavy marketing sites, and reliable hosting; frequent criticism focuses on the complexity and cost of its pricing structure as sites scale, a steeper learning curve than simpler builders, and the difficulty of fully migrating a site's content and design off the platform once built. For a directory audience, Webflow is best framed as the design-forward, professional-grade no-code website builder of choice for agencies and marketing teams, positioned above Wix, Squarespace, and Webnode in flexibility and price, while remaining friendlier to non-developers than a fully custom-coded site.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Webflow
paragraphs: Webflow is a visual website builder founded in 2012 and based in San Francisco, California, built around the idea that designers should be able to build production-ready sites visually without sacrificing clean, semantic code underneath.

Instead of locking designs into a proprietary rendering engine, Webflow outputs real HTML and CSS, which is one reason it is popular with agencies and design-led marketing teams that care about performance and long-term flexibility.
The platform pairs its visual canvas with a built-in CMS, global hosting, and, more recently, AI-assisted tools for generating and editing site content and layouts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Webflow's core is a visual canvas with full control over CSS properties, so designers can build fully custom layouts, interactions, and animations without writing code, while developers can still drop in custom code or code components where needed.
The built-in CMS, organized as Collections, supports content-heavy sites such as blogs, directories, and marketing resource hubs, with dynamic templates that update automatically as content changes.
Webflow Ecommerce adds product catalogs, cart, and checkout, and the platform includes localization tools for multilingual sites, forms, site search, and hosting on a global content delivery network.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Webflow overhauled its pricing in May 2026, replacing its older CMS and Business site tiers with a single Premium plan and simplifying the overall lineup to Starter, Basic, Premium, and Enterprise.

The Basic plan starts at around 15 dollars per month billed annually for simpler marketing sites, while Premium, at around 25 dollars per month billed annually, adds CMS collections, site search, and higher bandwidth and page limits.

On top of Site plans, Workspace seats, Full, Limited, and Free, are billed separately and control collaborator access, and a new Team plan bundles seats, CMS collections, and publishing workflows for organizations that need more structure than self-serve plans provide.

features.title: Visual Canvas with Full CSS Control
features.description: Build custom, responsive layouts visually while Webflow outputs clean, semantic HTML and CSS.

features.title: Built-in CMS (Collections)
features.description: Dynamic content management for blogs, directories, and other structured content types.

features.title: Ecommerce
features.description: Product catalogs, cart, and checkout tools for running an online store directly on Webflow.

features.title: Global CDN Hosting
features.description: Built-in hosting and content delivery included with every Site plan.

features.title: Interactions and Animations
features.description: Visual tools for scroll-based, hover, and click-triggered animations without JavaScript.

features.title: AI-Assisted Site Building
features.description: AI credits and tools bundled into Workspace plans for generating layouts and content.

features.title: Custom Code and Code Components
features.description: Ability to embed custom code or reusable code-based components for advanced functionality.

features.title: Localization
features.description: Tools for building and managing multilingual versions of a site from one project.

pros: Produces clean, semantic HTML and CSS instead of bloated builder markup
Far more design flexibility than Wix, Squarespace, or Webnode
Strong CMS for blogs, directories, and content-heavy marketing sites
Reliable built-in hosting on a global CDN
Large ecosystem of templates and a professional community of agencies and freelancers
cons: Pricing structure, combining site plans, workspace seats, and bandwidth or CMS add-ons, can get expensive at scale
Steeper learning curve than simple drag-and-drop builders
Ecommerce functionality is less mature than dedicated platforms like Shopify
Fully exporting a site's design and CMS content to leave the platform is difficult

faqs.question: Is Webflow free to use?
faqs.answer: Webflow offers a free Starter Site plan with limited pages and a Webflow-hosted subdomain, but a paid plan is needed for a custom domain and most advanced features.

faqs.question: How much does Webflow cost in 2026?
faqs.answer: After the May 2026 pricing update, Site plans start around 15 dollars a month for Basic and 25 dollars a month for Premium when billed annually, with Enterprise available at custom pricing.

faqs.question: What changed with Webflow's May 2026 pricing update?
faqs.answer: Webflow consolidated its old CMS and Business site plans into a single Premium plan, introduced a new Team plan, and began bundling AI credits into all Workspace plans.

faqs.question: Can I use Webflow without knowing how to code?
faqs.answer: Yes, Webflow is designed as a no-code visual builder, though it also supports custom code for teams that want more control.

faqs.question: Does Webflow support ecommerce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Webflow Ecommerce provides product catalogs, cart, and checkout, though it is generally less feature-rich than dedicated ecommerce platforms.

faqs.question: Can I add custom code to a Webflow site?
faqs.answer: Yes, Webflow supports embedded custom code and code components for functionality beyond the visual builder.

faqs.question: How is Webflow different from WordPress?
faqs.answer: Webflow is a hosted, visual, no-code platform with built-in hosting and CMS, while WordPress is open-source software that typically requires separate hosting, plugins, and more technical maintenance.

faqs.question: Who is Webflow best for?
faqs.answer: Webflow is best suited to design agencies, marketing teams, and freelance designers who want strong visual control and a built-in CMS without managing custom code and infrastructure.

use_cases.title: Agency client websites
use_cases.description: Agencies design and deliver fully custom, responsive client sites without hand-coding every project.

use_cases.title: Marketing sites with a blog and CMS
use_cases.description: Startups and marketing teams manage a company site alongside a dynamic, content-managed blog.

use_cases.title: Ecommerce storefronts
use_cases.description: Small to mid-size retailers run a design-forward online store using Webflow's built-in ecommerce tools.

features: 20,000 CMS items across up to 40 collections

enrichment.short_description: Webhook.site is a free developer tool that instantly generates a unique URL and email address so you can inspect, log, and debug incoming HTTP requests, webhooks, and emails in real time.
enrichment.seo_title: Webhook.site Review: Features, Pricing, and How It Works
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Webhook.site lets developers test and debug webhooks and HTTP requests for free, plus its Pro and Enterprise pricing and open-source self-hosted option.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Webhook.site is a free, developer-focused utility for testing, inspecting, and debugging incoming HTTP requests, webhooks, and emails. Built by developer Simon Fredsted using Laravel for the backend API and Angular.js for the frontend, the tool is also released as an open-source, MIT-licensed project on GitHub that can be self-hosted with Docker, in addition to the hosted cloud version at webhook.site. The core workflow is simple: visiting the site instantly generates a unique, disposable URL and a matching email address; any HTTP request sent to that URL, or any email sent to that address, is captured and displayed in a live, browser-based inspector showing headers, body, query parameters, and timing. This makes it a common first stop for developers integrating with services that send webhooks, such as Stripe, GitHub, or Shopify, since it removes the need to spin up a local server or use tunneling tools just to see what a webhook payload looks like. Beyond basic inspection, Webhook.site supports configuring custom responses, including status codes, headers, and bodies, so a URL can simulate a real API endpoint during testing, and its Pro tier adds Custom Actions, a scripting feature that lets users transform, forward, or react to incoming requests programmatically, effectively turning a test URL into a lightweight webhook processing pipeline. On the free plan, each URL is limited to 50 stored requests and is automatically deleted after 7 days, which is sufficient for quick, one-off debugging but not for long-running integrations or team use. The paid Pro plan, priced at 27 dollars per month or 270 dollars per year, raises those limits substantially, adds Custom Actions, CSV export, and URLs that no longer expire, while the Enterprise plan, at 69 dollars per month or 699 dollars per year, adds custom domain support, SAML single sign-on, multi-user support, and a much larger request history. Webhook.site competes with tools like Beeceptor and Hookdeck, but is generally regarded as one of the fastest and simplest options for ad hoc webhook inspection, especially given its no-signup free tier and open-source self-hosting option for teams with data-residency requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Webhook.site
paragraphs: Webhook.site is a lightweight developer tool that gives you an instant, disposable URL and email address for capturing and inspecting incoming HTTP requests, webhooks, and emails.
It was built by developer Simon Fredsted and is offered both as a hosted cloud service at webhook.site and as an open-source, MIT-licensed project that can be self-hosted with Docker.

Developers use it as a fast alternative to spinning up a local server or tunneling tool whenever they need to see exactly what an external service, such as a payment processor or a source-control platform, sends in a webhook payload.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core feature is the live request inspector, which shows full headers, body content, query parameters, and timing for every request or email sent to a generated URL or address, updating in real time in the browser.
Custom response configuration lets a URL act as a mock API endpoint, returning a chosen status code, headers, and body, which is useful for testing how another system handles different responses.

The Pro-tier Custom Actions feature adds scripting so incoming requests can be transformed, forwarded, or reacted to programmatically, moving Webhook.site beyond pure inspection into lightweight webhook processing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Webhook.site is free to use for quick testing, with each free URL limited to 50 stored requests and automatic data deletion after 7 days.

The Pro plan costs 27 dollars per month or 270 dollars per year and raises limits to up to 50 custom URLs, unlimited requests with a 10,000-request history, Custom Actions, CSV export, and URLs that no longer expire.

The Enterprise plan costs 69 dollars per month or 699 dollars per year and adds unlimited URLs and schedules, a 100,000-request history, custom domain support, SAML SSO, multi-user support, and priority support.

features.title: Instant Disposable URLs and Email Addresses
features.description: Generates a unique URL and email address immediately, with no signup required for basic use.

features.title: Live Request Inspector
features.description: Displays headers, body, query parameters, and timing for every captured request in real time.

features.title: Custom Response Simulation
features.description: Lets a URL respond with a configured status code, headers, and body to mimic a real API endpoint.

features.title: Custom Actions (Pro)
features.description: Scripting feature for transforming, forwarding, or reacting to incoming requests programmatically.

features.title: Request History and Export
features.description: Stores request history up to plan limits, with CSV export available on paid tiers.

features.title: Team and Multi-User Support
features.description: Enterprise plans add multi-user access and SAML SSO for shared team use.

features.title: Open-Source Self-Hosting
features.description: An MIT-licensed version can be self-hosted with Docker for teams with data-residency needs.

features.title: API Access
features.description: Programmatic access for integrating Webhook.site capture and inspection into automated test suites.

pros: Free, instant, and requires no signup for basic use
Open-source and self-hostable for teams with data-residency needs
Simple, fast interface purpose-built for webhook and API debugging
Custom Actions let Pro users script real webhook logic, not just view requests
cons: Free tier data expires after 7 days and caps at only 50 requests per URL
Not a full API mocking or production webhook-delivery platform
Advanced features like custom domains and SSO require the paid Enterprise tier
No built-in retry, queueing, or delivery-guarantee features for production traffic

faqs.question: What is Webhook.site used for?
faqs.answer: It is used to instantly capture and inspect incoming HTTP requests, webhooks, and emails, most often while integrating with or debugging a third-party API.

faqs.question: Is Webhook.site free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core inspection feature is free, with a limit of 50 stored requests per URL and 7-day data retention on the free plan.

faqs.question: How long does data stay on Webhook.site?
faqs.answer: On the free plan, URLs and their captured data are automatically deleted after 7 days; paid plans offer URLs that do not expire.

faqs.question: Can Webhook.site be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, an open-source, MIT-licensed version is available on GitHub and can be self-hosted using Docker.

faqs.question: Is it safe to send sensitive data to Webhook.site?
faqs.answer: The hosted free and Pro tiers are intended for testing rather than sensitive production data; teams with strict data requirements often use the self-hosted open-source version instead.

faqs.question: What does the Pro plan add?
faqs.answer: Pro raises request limits, adds Custom Actions scripting, CSV export, and URLs that never expire, for 27 dollars per month.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Webhook.site?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Beeceptor and Hookdeck, which offer similar request inspection and, in some cases, production-grade webhook delivery features.

faqs.question: Does Webhook.site support custom domains?
faqs.answer: Custom domain support for URLs and emails is available on the Enterprise plan.

use_cases.title: Testing third-party webhook integrations
use_cases.description: Capturing and inspecting webhook payloads from services like Stripe, GitHub, or Shopify during integration work.

use_cases.title: API and email debugging during QA
use_cases.description: Verifying that an application sends the correct requests or emails during manual or automated testing.

use_cases.title: Prototyping webhook-driven workflows
use_cases.description: Using Custom Actions to simulate and script how an internal system should react to incoming webhook data.
use_cases.audience: developers building internal automation and integrations

features: Up to 50 custom URLs and 5 schedules
Custom domain support for URLs and emails
Priority support and dedicated queue resources

enrichment.short_description: WebinarJam is a cloud-based webinar hosting platform built by Genesis Digital that lets marketers run live, automated, and hybrid webinars with built-in registration pages, email and SMS reminders, and sales tools.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See WebinarJam's live and automated webinar features, pricing plans by attendee limit, and how it compares to other webinar software in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WebinarJam is a cloud-based webinar hosting platform designed for marketers, coaches, and course creators who use live and automated webinars as a sales and lead-generation channel. It is a flagship product of Genesis Digital, also known for Kartra, a company whose first product was WebinarJam and which has been building direct-to-consumer marketing software since 2013. The platform runs webinars in a web browser without requiring attendees to install software, and it bundles the full funnel around a webinar into one tool: customizable registration and thank-you pages, automated email and SMS reminder sequences, live chat and moderation, polls and surveys, a built-in whiteboard, video injections that play pre-recorded clips as if live, live offer displays for driving purchases during the presentation, and the ability to simulcast to Facebook and YouTube. Higher-tier plans bundle EverWebinar, WebinarJam's companion product for fully automated, evergreen webinars that replay on a schedule and can simulate live chat and attendee activity. WebinarJam is priced by attendee capacity and account features rather than by number of webinars, with four tiers: Starter, supporting 100 attendees with 1 host and 1-hour sessions; Basic, supporting 500 attendees with 2 hosts, 2-hour sessions, and EverWebinar included; Professional, supporting 2,000 attendees with 4 hosts, 3-hour sessions, and EverWebinar included; and Enterprise, supporting 5,000 attendees with 6 hosts, 4-hour sessions, and EverWebinar included, with annual billing offering meaningful savings over monthly pricing. New users can try the platform for 14 days for 1 dollar, and paid subscriptions carry a 30-day money-back guarantee. WebinarJam is most commonly used by online course creators, coaches, agencies, and SaaS companies running product demo webinars as part of a launch or evergreen sales funnel, and it competes with tools like Zoom Webinar, Demio, and WebinarNinja, generally differentiating itself through built-in marketing and sales features rather than positioning itself as a pure video-conferencing tool. Reviewers commonly praise its all-in-one funnel approach and reliability for larger live audiences, while common criticisms include an interface that feels dated compared to newer competitors, a learning curve around its many settings, and pricing that scales quickly once a business needs higher attendee caps or more hosts.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WebinarJam
paragraphs: WebinarJam is a browser-based webinar hosting platform built by Genesis Digital, a company founded in 2013 whose first product was WebinarJam, alongside its later product Kartra.

Unlike a plain video-conferencing tool, WebinarJam is built specifically around the webinar-as-sales-funnel model, bundling registration pages, reminder automation, and in-webinar sales tools into one product.
It supports both live webinars and, via its companion product EverWebinar included on most paid plans, fully automated evergreen webinars that replay on a schedule.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Every plan includes customizable registration and thank-you pages, automated email and SMS reminder sequences, live chat and moderation tools, polls and surveys, and a built-in whiteboard for presentations.

Sales-focused features include live offer displays that let presenters show a time-limited purchase button during the webinar, and video injections that play pre-recorded segments seamlessly within a live session.
Higher tiers add more hosts, longer maximum session lengths, more team member seats, and support for larger attendee counts, up to 5,000 on the top Enterprise plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: WebinarJam offers a 14-day trial for 1 dollar, followed by four paid tiers priced primarily by attendee capacity: Starter, Basic, Professional, and Enterprise.
Annual billing runs roughly 20 percent cheaper than monthly billing across all tiers, and every paid plan carries a 30-day money-back guarantee.
EverWebinar, the automated webinar companion product, is bundled into the Basic tier and above, so businesses that need evergreen funnels generally need at least the second-lowest plan.

features.title: Live and Automated Webinars
features.description: Supports both real-time live webinars and, via bundled EverWebinar, fully automated evergreen replays.

features.title: Built-in Registration and Thank-You Pages
features.description: Customizable pages for capturing registrants and confirming sign-ups without a separate landing page tool.

features.title: Email and SMS Reminder Automation
features.description: Automated reminder sequences sent by email and SMS ahead of and during scheduled webinars.

features.title: Live Chat, Polls, and Surveys
features.description: Real-time audience interaction tools for engagement and moderation during live sessions.

features.title: Video Injections and Offers
features.description: Plays pre-recorded video segments as if live and displays time-limited purchase offers during the presentation.

features.title: Multi-Host Support
features.description: Multiple presenters can co-host a session, with host limits increasing on higher-tier plans.

features.title: Social Media Simulcasting
features.description: Streams webinars simultaneously to Facebook and YouTube alongside the WebinarJam room.

features.title: EverWebinar Integration
features.description: Bundled on Basic and higher plans, enabling fully automated evergreen webinar funnels.

pros: All-in-one webinar funnel combining registration, reminders, the live event, and sales offers in one tool
Automated or evergreen webinars available via bundled EverWebinar on most plans
Supports large live audiences up to 5,000 attendees on the top tier
30-day money-back guarantee plus a low-cost 14-day trial
cons: Interface and design feel dated compared to newer webinar tools
Pricing scales quickly with attendee count and number of hosts
Session length caps, from 1 to 4 hours depending on plan, can be restrictive for long-form training
Key features like EverWebinar and more hosts require moving to mid or higher tiers

faqs.question: What is WebinarJam used for?
faqs.answer: WebinarJam is used to host live and automated webinars for product launches, sales presentations, and online course delivery, with built-in registration and marketing tools.

faqs.question: How much does WebinarJam cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 39 dollars per month billed annually for the Starter tier and scale up to 379 dollars per month billed annually for the Enterprise tier, based mainly on attendee capacity.

faqs.question: Does WebinarJam include automated webinars?
faqs.answer: Yes, its companion product EverWebinar for automated evergreen webinars is bundled into the Basic plan and above.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial?
faqs.answer: WebinarJam offers a 14-day trial for 1 dollar rather than a fully free tier.

faqs.question: How many attendees can WebinarJam handle?
faqs.answer: Attendee capacity ranges from 100 on the Starter plan up to 5,000 on the Enterprise plan.

faqs.question: What is the difference between WebinarJam and EverWebinar?
faqs.answer: WebinarJam hosts live, real-time webinars, while EverWebinar plays fully automated, pre-recorded webinars on a repeating schedule that can simulate live chat activity.

faqs.question: Who makes WebinarJam?
faqs.answer: WebinarJam is built by Genesis Digital, a company whose first product was WebinarJam and which also makes Kartra.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to WebinarJam?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Zoom Webinar, Demio, and WebinarNinja.

enrichment.short_description: Webnode is a budget-friendly, drag-and-drop website builder aimed at individuals and small businesses, offering multilingual sites, a free plan, and simple ecommerce tools.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid plans from around $5.50/month billed annually
enrichment.headquarters: Brno, Czech Republic (part of team.blue, headquartered in Ghent, Belgium)
enrichment.seo_title: Webnode Review 2026: Features, Pricing, and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Webnode's website builder works, its free and paid pricing plans, multilingual site support, and how it compares to Wix and other budget builders.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Webnode is a cloud-based, drag-and-drop website builder that launched in 2008 and has grown into one of Europe's widely used self-serve site-building tools, particularly popular for personal sites, portfolios, small business brochure sites, and simple online stores. The company was originally founded as Webnode AG in Switzerland, with its main development and operations base in Brno, Czech Republic; in 2020 Webnode became part of team.blue, a pan-European digital services group headquartered in Ghent, Belgium, that also owns other hosting and domain brands. Webnode's positioning is squarely budget-focused: it competes with Wix, Weebly, and Squarespace on ease of use, but undercuts most of them on price, and it is particularly known for strong multilingual website support, letting users build and manage a single site in multiple languages from one dashboard, which appeals to its large user base across Europe and Latin America. The builder uses a visual drag-and-drop editor with pre-built templates organized by industry, and it includes tools for blogging, contact forms, basic SEO settings, and a lightweight ecommerce module for selling a limited catalog of products with integrated payment gateways. Webnode operates on a freemium model: a free plan lets users build and publish a site on a Webnode subdomain with Webnode branding and limited storage, while paid tiers remove ads, add custom domain support, increase storage and bandwidth, and unlock ecommerce and additional design features. Published pricing tiers commonly cited include a Limited plan around 5.50 dollars per month, a Mini plan around 10 dollars per month, a Standard plan around 16.90 dollars per month, and a Profi plan around 26.50 dollars per month, typically discounted for annual billing; exact pricing varies by region and currency. Webnode is best suited for individuals, freelancers, and small local businesses that want a straightforward, low-cost way to get a professional-looking multilingual website online without the design depth of Webflow or the broader app ecosystem of Wix, and reviewers generally describe it as reliable and easy to use, with fewer advanced customization options than higher-end no-code builders.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Webnode
paragraphs: Webnode is a cloud-based website builder that launched in 2008, originally as Webnode AG in Switzerland, with its main development and operations base in Brno, Czech Republic.
In 2020 Webnode became part of team.blue, a large pan-European digital services group headquartered in Ghent, Belgium, that owns several hosting and domain brands across the continent.

The platform is positioned as a budget-friendly alternative to Wix and Squarespace, particularly known for strong multilingual site support that appeals to its large European and Latin American user base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Webnode's visual drag-and-drop editor uses pre-built, industry-specific templates, making it straightforward for non-technical users to launch a site quickly.
A standout feature is multilingual site management, letting a single site serve content in multiple languages from one dashboard rather than requiring separate installations.
The platform also includes blogging tools, contact forms, basic SEO settings, and a lightweight ecommerce module suited to a limited product catalog with integrated payment gateways.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Webnode offers a free plan that includes a Webnode subdomain, Webnode branding, and limited storage, with no time limit on use.

Paid tiers commonly cited include Limited around 5.50 dollars per month, Mini around 10 dollars per month, Standard around 16.90 dollars per month, and Profi around 26.50 dollars per month, generally billed annually for the lowest rate.
Higher tiers progressively add custom domain support, more storage and bandwidth, and ecommerce features, with exact pricing varying somewhat by region and currency.

Free plan available with no time limit
Strong built-in multilingual site support
Simple, beginner-friendly editor
Backed by team.blue, a large established European hosting group
cons: Design flexibility is limited compared to Webflow or Wix
Free and lower tiers carry Webnode branding and limited storage or bandwidth
Ecommerce features are basic and not suited for larger catalogs
Fewer third-party app or integration options than larger competitors

faqs.question: Is Webnode free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Webnode offers a free plan with a Webnode subdomain and Webnode branding, though a paid plan is needed for a custom domain and more features.

faqs.question: How much does Webnode cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans commonly start around 5.50 dollars per month and range up to around 26.50 dollars per month for the highest tier, billed annually.

faqs.question: Does Webnode support multiple languages on one site?
faqs.answer: Yes, multilingual site management is one of Webnode's core features, letting one site serve content in several languages.

faqs.question: Can I sell products with Webnode?
faqs.answer: Yes, higher-tier plans include a lightweight ecommerce module suited to a limited product catalog.

faqs.question: Who owns Webnode?
faqs.answer: Webnode has been part of team.blue, a pan-European digital services group headquartered in Ghent, Belgium, since 2020.

faqs.question: How does Webnode compare to Wix?
faqs.answer: Webnode is generally cheaper and simpler than Wix but offers less design flexibility and a smaller app ecosystem.

faqs.question: Can I use a custom domain on Webnode?
faqs.answer: Yes, custom domain support is available starting on Webnode's paid plans.

faqs.question: Is Webnode good for beginners?
faqs.answer: Yes, its drag-and-drop editor and templates are designed to be approachable for users with no coding or design experience.

enrichment.short_description: Webrecorder is an open-source suite of web archiving tools, including ArchiveWeb.page, Browsertrix, and ReplayWeb.page, that lets anyone capture, verify, and replay interactive copies of web pages.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open-source tools); Browsertrix Cloud hosting from $30/month
enrichment.seo_title: Webrecorder Review: Open-Source Web Archiving Tools and Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how Webrecorder's open-source tools, ArchiveWeb.page, Browsertrix, and ReplayWeb.page, capture and replay interactive web archives, plus Browsertrix Cloud pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Webrecorder is an open-source project and small technology company dedicated to building tools for high-fidelity web archiving. Its origins trace back to developer Ilya Kreymer's early browser-based archiving prototypes, which were adopted and funded starting in 2016 by Rhizome, a New York-based digital arts nonprofit, through a multi-year grant from the Andrew W. Mellon Foundation. After several years of development under Rhizome, the project split in 2020: the hosted archiving service was renamed Conifer and remained with Rhizome, while Kreymer founded the independent Webrecorder Software LLC, based in the United States, to continue developing the underlying open-source tools. Unlike traditional web archiving approaches such as simple HTML scraping or crawl-based snapshots, Webrecorder's tools focus on capturing pages as they actually behave when browsed, including JavaScript-rendered content, video and audio playback, scrolling, and interactive elements, producing archives that can be faithfully replayed later even if the original site changes or disappears. The toolset includes ArchiveWeb.page, a browser extension and desktop app for capturing pages as you browse them; ReplayWeb.page, a browser-based viewer for replaying archived content without needing a server; pywb, a Python library for web archive replay; and Browsertrix, a cloud-based, high-fidelity crawling service for archiving entire websites at scale, aimed at libraries, archives, researchers, and organizations with compliance or legal-hold needs. Webrecorder also maintains and champions the WACZ file format, an open standard for packaging and sharing web archives. Most of Webrecorder's tools, including ArchiveWeb.page and ReplayWeb.page, are free and open source and can be self-hosted or run entirely client-side in a browser. Browsertrix, the hosted crawling service, offers a free multi-day trial followed by paid plans starting around 30 dollars per month for a standard tier that includes several hundred minutes of crawl execution time, hundreds of gigabytes of storage, multiple concurrent crawls, and per-crawl page limits, with higher tiers and custom plans available for larger archiving programs. Webrecorder is widely used by librarians, digital preservationists, journalists, researchers, and legal teams who need to capture an authentic, interactive record of a web page for evidence, research, or long-term preservation, positioning it as a specialized, standards-based alternative to generic screenshot tools or crawl-based archives.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Webrecorder
paragraphs: Webrecorder is an open-source project focused on high-fidelity web archiving, tracing back to developer Ilya Kreymer's early browser-based archiving prototypes, which were funded and developed at the nonprofit Rhizome starting in 2016.

In 2020, the project split: the hosted archiving service became Conifer under Rhizome, while Kreymer founded Webrecorder Software LLC in the United States to continue building the underlying open-source tools.

Rather than taking static snapshots, Webrecorder's tools capture pages as they actually behave in a browser, including scripts, video, audio, and interactive elements, so archived copies replay just as the original did.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ArchiveWeb.page is a browser extension and desktop app that lets anyone capture pages as they browse, while ReplayWeb.page is a browser-based viewer that can replay archived content without needing a server.

Browsertrix is Webrecorder's cloud-based crawling service, designed to archive entire websites at scale for organizations that need automated, high-fidelity captures rather than manual page-by-page recording.
Webrecorder maintains the WACZ file format, an open standard for packaging web archives, along with pywb, a Python library used to power replay for archives created with its tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Webrecorder's core tools, ArchiveWeb.page, ReplayWeb.page, and pywb, are free and open source, and can be self-hosted or run entirely in the browser without an account.

Browsertrix, the hosted crawling service, offers a free trial period before converting to a paid Standard tier priced around 30 dollars per month, which includes several hundred minutes of crawl time, hundreds of gigabytes of storage, and multiple concurrent crawls.
Larger organizations with bigger crawl volumes or dedicated support needs can arrange custom Browsertrix pricing above the Standard tier.

features.title: Self-Hosting and Open-Source Licensing
features.description: Core tools can be self-hosted or run client-side, avoiding dependence on a hosted service.

features.title: Collections and Sharing
features.description: Tools for organizing archived pages into collections that can be shared or published.

pros: Captures fully interactive, JavaScript-heavy pages rather than static snapshots
Core tools are free, open-source, and self-hostable
The WACZ format provides an open, portable standard for archived content
Browsertrix offers scalable, managed crawling for teams that need it
Trusted by libraries, journalists, and legal or compliance teams for evidentiary-quality archives
cons: Browsertrix's paid tiers are needed for large-scale or automated crawling
Tools are aimed at technical or archival users, less approachable for casual users than a general-purpose archive site
Self-hosting the more advanced crawling infrastructure requires technical setup
Smaller company and community than major commercial archiving vendors

faqs.question: What is Webrecorder used for?
faqs.answer: Webrecorder is used to capture, preserve, and replay interactive copies of web pages, commonly for legal evidence, research, journalism, and library archiving.

faqs.question: Is Webrecorder free?
faqs.answer: Its core tools, ArchiveWeb.page, ReplayWeb.page, and pywb, are free and open source; the hosted Browsertrix crawling service has paid plans starting around 30 dollars per month.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ArchiveWeb.page and Browsertrix?
faqs.answer: ArchiveWeb.page is a browser tool for manually capturing pages as you browse them, while Browsertrix is a cloud crawling service for automatically archiving entire websites at scale.

faqs.question: What is the WACZ file format?
faqs.answer: WACZ is an open, standardized file format that Webrecorder maintains for packaging and sharing web archive collections.

faqs.question: Can Webrecorder capture JavaScript-heavy or interactive pages?
faqs.answer: Yes, its tools are specifically built to capture dynamic, script-driven content, video, audio, and user interactions, not just static HTML.

faqs.question: Is Webrecorder open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its core tools are open source, and most can be self-hosted or run entirely client-side.

faqs.question: How does Webrecorder compare to generic web archiving services?
faqs.answer: Webrecorder focuses on high-fidelity, interactive capture and open standards like WACZ, making it better suited to evidentiary or research-grade archiving than simple crawl-based snapshot tools.

faqs.question: Who uses Webrecorder?
faqs.answer: Librarians, digital archivists, journalists, researchers, and legal or compliance teams are among its most common users.

enrichment.short_description: Webstudio is an open-source, no-code visual website builder positioned as a Webflow alternative, letting teams design with full CSS control while connecting to any headless CMS and self-hosting if they choose.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Hobby plan); Pro plan from $15/month billed yearly
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Webstudio's open-source visual builder compares to Webflow, including its free Hobby plan, Pro and Team pricing, and headless CMS integrations.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Webstudio is an open-source, no-code visual website builder built by Webstudio, Inc., a company incorporated in Dublin, Ireland, and founded in 2022 by Oleg Isonen, a former Webflow engineer who left Webflow in 2021 to build an open alternative to the closed, proprietary site builders dominating the no-code market. Webstudio's core positioning is as a direct, more open competitor to Webflow: it offers a similar visual canvas with full control over CSS properties, but its underlying code is released under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license and published on GitHub, meaning the builder itself, and the sites created with it, can be self-hosted on a developer's own infrastructure rather than being locked into a single vendor's cloud. Unlike many website builders that bundle their own proprietary CMS, Webstudio is designed to connect to external headless content sources such as Strapi, Supabase, Notion, and WordPress, giving teams flexibility to manage content outside the builder. Webstudio uses a freemium pricing model built around its own hosted cloud offering: a free Hobby plan lets users build unlimited projects and pages on a wstd.io subdomain with limited asset storage, while the paid Pro plan, priced around 15 dollars per month when billed yearly, adds custom domains, roughly 100,000 monthly page views, unlimited form submissions, a client-friendly Content Mode, staging environments with backups, and CMS integrations; a Team plan, around 35 dollars per month billed yearly, adds workspaces, member roles, and five included seats for collaborative projects. Because the core builder is open source, technical teams can bypass Webstudio's hosted pricing entirely and self-host both the visual editor and the sites it produces, which is a key differentiator from closed-source builders like Webflow, Wix, or Webnode. Webstudio is best suited for developers, technical marketers, and agencies who want Webflow-style visual design speed without vendor lock-in, and who value being able to inspect, modify, or self-host the underlying platform. As a newer entrant that has been publicly building since around 2022, its template library, third-party integration ecosystem, and market presence remain smaller than Webflow's, though it has drawn attention in the no-code and open-source communities specifically as an alternative to proprietary builder lock-in.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Webstudio
paragraphs: Webstudio is an open-source, no-code visual website builder founded in 2022 by Oleg Isonen, a former Webflow engineer, and incorporated as Webstudio, Inc. in Dublin, Ireland.

It was built specifically as an open alternative to closed, proprietary no-code builders, offering a visual canvas comparable to Webflow's while releasing its core code under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license on GitHub.
Because the builder itself is open source, both the editor and the sites it produces can be self-hosted on a developer's own infrastructure instead of being locked into a single vendor's cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Webstudio's visual canvas gives designers full control over CSS properties, similar in spirit to Webflow, letting teams build custom, responsive layouts without writing code by hand.

Rather than bundling a proprietary CMS, Webstudio connects to external headless content sources such as Strapi, Supabase, Notion, and WordPress, so teams can manage content independently of the builder.
A Content Mode allows non-technical clients to edit page content safely without touching design, while staging environments with backups support safer iteration for paid plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The free Hobby plan supports unlimited projects and pages on a wstd.io subdomain with 250MB of asset storage, useful for personal projects or evaluation.

The Pro plan, around 15 dollars per month billed yearly, adds custom domains, roughly 100,000 monthly page views, unlimited form submissions, Content Mode, staging environments, and CMS integrations, with 20GB of asset storage.

The Team plan, around 35 dollars per month billed yearly, includes everything in Pro plus workspaces, member roles, and five included seats, and because the core is open source, technical teams can also self-host instead of paying for hosted plans at all.

features.description: Supports safer iteration with staging and backup capabilities on paid plans.

features.description: Team plan adds shared workspaces, member roles, and multiple included seats.

features.title: Code Export
features.description: Projects can be shared or exported, supporting portability outside the hosted platform.

pros: Open-source and self-hostable, avoiding the vendor lock-in of closed builders
Full CSS control similar to Webflow's design depth
Connects to external headless CMS platforms instead of forcing a proprietary CMS
Generous free Hobby plan for unlimited projects and pages
Built by a team with direct Webflow product experience
cons: Smaller template and integration ecosystem than Webflow
Self-hosting requires technical setup and ongoing maintenance
Newer platform with a smaller community and less third-party documentation
Page-view overages on the Pro plan add cost as traffic scales

faqs.question: Is Webstudio free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Hobby plan is free and includes unlimited projects and pages on a wstd.io subdomain, though a paid plan is needed for a custom domain and more storage.

faqs.question: Is Webstudio really open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, its core code is released under the AGPL-3.0-or-later license and published publicly on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can Webstudio be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, because the builder is open source, both the editor and the sites created with it can be self-hosted on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: How is Webstudio different from Webflow?
faqs.answer: Webstudio offers similar visual design capabilities but is open source and self-hostable, while Webflow is closed-source and only available as a hosted cloud product.

faqs.question: Does Webstudio support headless CMS platforms?
faqs.answer: Yes, it connects to external headless CMS sources including Strapi, Supabase, Notion, and WordPress.

faqs.question: What does the Pro plan include?
faqs.answer: Pro, around 15 dollars per month billed yearly, adds custom domains, about 100,000 monthly page views, unlimited form submissions, Content Mode, staging environments, and CMS integrations.

faqs.question: Who built Webstudio?
faqs.answer: Webstudio was founded in 2022 by Oleg Isonen, a former Webflow engineer, and is incorporated as Webstudio, Inc. in Dublin, Ireland.

faqs.question: Is Webstudio good for agencies?
faqs.answer: It can suit agencies that want Webflow-style design flexibility without vendor lock-in, though its template and integration ecosystem is still smaller than Webflow's.

enrichment.short_description: WebThings Gateway is a free, open-source smart home hub software that lets you self-host a private, local Internet of Things gateway for monitoring and controlling connected devices without depending on a manufacturer's cloud.
enrichment.seo_title: WebThings Gateway Review: Open-Source Smart Home Hub
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how WebThings Gateway, the open-source IoT hub originally built by Mozilla, lets you self-host a private smart home gateway, plus its features and add-ons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: WebThings Gateway is a free, open-source software distribution for building a self-hosted smart home or IoT gateway, focused on privacy, security, and interoperability rather than tying users to a single manufacturer's cloud service. The project originated inside Mozilla as Project Things, first released in June 2017 following an internal whitepaper exploring how Mozilla's mission around user privacy and an open web could extend to the emerging Internet of Things ecosystem. Over roughly four years of development at Mozilla, the project evolved into the WebThings platform, combining the WebThings Framework, for implementing the Web of Things standard on devices, with the WebThings Gateway, the hub application end users run, typically on a Raspberry Pi or similar hardware. By the time of its 1.0 release, WebThings Gateway had been translated into around 34 languages, downloaded over 100,000 times, and supported by more than a hundred community-built add-ons that bridge protocols like Zigbee, Z-Wave, and Philips Hue into a single standardized web-based interface. In December 2020, Mozilla spun the project out of its own umbrella, and WebThings became an independent, community-led open-source project with its own home at webthings.io; UK-based company Krellian, founded in 2020 by former Mozilla and Google engineer Ben Francis, took on sponsorship of the WebThings website and cloud infrastructure, including automatic software updates and a secure remote access service for gateways. Because it is free and open source, WebThings Gateway can be installed on your own hardware, most commonly a Raspberry Pi, and gives users a local dashboard for monitoring sensors, controlling lights and switches, defining automation rules, and grouping devices, all without sending data to a third-party cloud unless the user explicitly opts into a remote access feature. In December 2025, the project shipped WebThings Gateway 2.0, adding a widely requested groups feature that lets users drag and drop devices into organizational groups. WebThings Gateway is best suited for privacy-conscious hobbyists, makers, and small organizations who want an open, standards-based alternative to closed ecosystems like Google Home, Amazon Alexa, or Samsung SmartThings, and who are comfortable with some self-hosting and hardware setup. It is not a polished consumer product sold through retail channels, but rather a community-maintained open-source project distributed as free software.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is WebThings Gateway
paragraphs: WebThings Gateway is open-source software for running your own private smart home or IoT hub, most commonly installed on a Raspberry Pi, that started life inside Mozilla in 2017 as Project Things.

The project was built around the idea of applying Mozilla's privacy-focused, open-web philosophy to the Internet of Things, implementing the Web of Things standard so devices from different manufacturers can be controlled through one consistent interface.

In December 2020, Mozilla spun the project out as an independent, community-led open-source effort, with UK-based Krellian, founded by former Mozilla and Google engineer Ben Francis, sponsoring the website and cloud infrastructure that keeps gateways updated and reachable remotely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The gateway provides a local dashboard for monitoring sensors, controlling lights, switches, and other connected devices, and defining automation rules, all running on hardware you control.
A large community add-on ecosystem, with more than a hundred add-ons at the project's peak, bridges protocols such as Zigbee, Z-Wave, and Philips Hue into the standardized Web of Things interface.
WebThings Gateway 2.0, released in December 2025, added a groups feature that lets users drag and drop devices into organizational groups for easier management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: WebThings Gateway is completely free and open source; there is no subscription fee to download, install, or run the software.
Users do need their own hardware, typically a Raspberry Pi, to host the gateway, which represents the main real-world cost of adoption.
An optional secure remote access service, sponsored by Krellian, allows access to a gateway from outside the local network without requiring complex router configuration.

features.title: Local, Self-Hosted Smart Home Hub
features.description: Runs entirely on your own hardware, keeping device control and data local by default.

features.title: Web of Things Standard Compliance
features.description: Implements the W3C Web of Things standard so different device types share one consistent interface.

features.title: Add-on Ecosystem
features.description: Community-built add-ons bridge protocols including Zigbee, Z-Wave, and Philips Hue into the gateway.

features.title: Rules Engine for Automation
features.description: Lets users define if-this-then-that style automation rules between connected devices.

features.title: Device Grouping
features.description: Added in version 2.0, lets users drag and drop devices into organizational groups.

features.title: Privacy-First Design
features.description: Does not require a mandatory third-party cloud account to operate the gateway locally.

features.title: Secure Remote Access
features.description: An optional service, sponsored by Krellian, provides remote access to a gateway without complex networking setup.

features.title: Multi-Language Interface
features.description: The gateway software has been translated into dozens of languages by its community.

pros: Free and fully open source, with no subscription required
Keeps smart home data local and private by default
Standards-based on the Web of Things rather than tied to one vendor's ecosystem
Backed by an active community and now maintained by Krellian following its spin-out from Mozilla
Broad device support through community-built add-ons
cons: Requires self-hosting on hardware like a Raspberry Pi, with more setup than plug-and-play hubs
Smaller device compatibility ecosystem than Google Home, Alexa, or SmartThings
Community-maintained project means slower feature velocity than well-funded commercial competitors
Less consumer-friendly onboarding and support than retail smart home products

faqs.question: Is WebThings Gateway free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is completely free and open source, with the only real cost being the hardware you run it on.

faqs.question: What hardware do I need to run WebThings Gateway?
faqs.answer: It is most commonly installed on a Raspberry Pi, though it can run on other compatible hardware as well.

faqs.question: Is WebThings Gateway still maintained after Mozilla left the project?
faqs.answer: Yes, it became an independent, community-led open-source project in December 2020, with Krellian sponsoring the website and cloud infrastructure, and it received a 2.0 release in December 2025.

faqs.question: What is the Web of Things standard?
faqs.answer: It is a W3C standard that WebThings Gateway implements so devices from different manufacturers can be controlled through one consistent web-based interface.

faqs.question: Does WebThings Gateway require a cloud account?
faqs.answer: No, the core gateway runs locally without a mandatory cloud account; a cloud-based remote access feature is optional.

faqs.question: What devices does WebThings Gateway support?
faqs.answer: Support depends on installed add-ons, which bridge protocols such as Zigbee, Z-Wave, and Philips Hue into the gateway.

faqs.question: How is WebThings Gateway different from Home Assistant?
faqs.answer: Both are open-source, self-hosted smart home platforms; WebThings Gateway is built specifically around the W3C Web of Things standard and originated at Mozilla, while Home Assistant has a broader independent integration ecosystem.

faqs.question: What is new in WebThings Gateway 2.0?
faqs.answer: Version 2.0, released in December 2025, added a groups feature that lets users organize devices by dragging and dropping them into groups.

use_cases.title: Privacy-focused home automation
use_cases.description: Running smart home automation entirely on local hardware without sending data to a third-party cloud.

use_cases.title: DIY Raspberry Pi smart hubs
use_cases.description: Building a personal, low-cost IoT gateway on a Raspberry Pi for home projects.

use_cases.title: Standards-based small-scale building automation
use_cases.description: Experimenting with or deploying Web of Things standards for small organizational or building automation projects.
